Novelty: first explicit use of LLM-derived textual semantics to steer fusion via text-to-vision cross-attention.
paper_title: Image Fusion via Vision-Language Model

problem: Obtaining robust, comprehensive semantics that can reliably guide fusion across diverse scenarios (infrared-visible, medical, multi-exposure, multi-focus).
method: A multi-granular text generation pipeline: extract image caption (BLIP2), dense caption (GRIT), and semantic masks (SAM), then feed these prompts to ChatGPT to produce detailed per-image paragraphs; fuse text in the textual domain (frozen BLIP2) before guiding vision. Novelty: combining VLM/VIS prompts with LLM summarization to create rich, task-aware textual guidance; validated via ablations.
paper_title: Image Fusion via Vision-Language Model

problem: Lack of benchmarks pairing fusion datasets with textual descriptions prevents progress on vision-language-based fusion.
method: VLF dataset: ChatGPT-generated paragraph descriptions (with manually refined prompts) for eight widely used fusion datasets across four tasks, providing paired text for all image pairs. Novelty: first vision-language resource tailored for image fusion to enable and standardize text-guided fusion research.
paper_title: Image Fusion via Vision-Language Model

problem: Choosing how many prompts to generate, how diverse they should be, and which LLM/CLIP scales to use affects performance and accessibility; robustness to distribution shifts must be preserved.
method: Ablations show accuracy scales with the number of LLM-prompt templates and with prompt diversity (higher LLM temperature), outperforming the baseline with as few as one LLM-prompt or 25 total image-prompts. CuPL’s gains hold across CLIP sizes and larger LLMs; lightweight alternatives (ChatGPT, GPT-J) with simple filtering still beat human prompts. Robustness is maintained or improved on ImageNet-V2 and Sketch; ensembling CuPL with standard prompts can further boost results.
paper_title: What does a platypus look like? Generating customized prompts for zero-shot image classification

problem: Conventional GPR migration relies on straight-line, grid-aligned scans and external positioning (e.g., GPS), leading to pseudo-3D imaging and poor usability indoors; arbitrary/random motion data cannot be accurately reconstructed without precise 6-DoF pose.
method: Fuse RGB-D visual odometry with IMU (visual–inertial fusion) to estimate the GPR sensor’s 6-DoF pose and integrate rotation-aware pose updates into a back-projection migration algorithm. By synchronizing A-scan data with poses, the method supports random motion trajectories and performs true 3D reconstruction via hemisphere intersections informed by the full pose, overcoming grid-scan constraints.
paper_title: GPR-based Subsurface Object Detection and Reconstruction Using Random Motion and DepthNet

problem: Background noise in GPR B-scans and unknown subsurface dielectric (permittivity) severely degrade migration and prevent accurate depth estimation of targets.
method: DepthNet: a DNN pipeline that (i) detects hyperbolic B-scan signatures with Faster R-CNN to define RoIs and discard out-of-RoI clutter for denoised migration, and (ii) predicts dielectric and per-target depth by combining a ResNet101-based dielectric estimator on raw B-scans with FC layers that fuse detection features and bounding boxes. A weighted loss jointly trains dielectric and depth prediction, providing migration with learned material properties and clean inputs for improved 3D reconstructions.
paper_title: GPR-based Subsurface Object Detection and Reconstruction Using Random Motion and DepthNet

problem: Projection-first deformable attention struggles to aggregate multi-view image features for 3D occupancy because fixed 3D reference points often project outside certain cameras and perspective causes depth-dependent scale inconsistency, hindering convergence.
method: Learning-first view attention: learn 3D sampling regions per voxel in a local view coordinate system (rotated by the query’s view angle) and project these learned 3D points to all cameras for multi-head attention aggregation, yielding rotation invariance, consistent 3D scale, and robust multi-view coverage.
paper_title: ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers

problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera perception is either limited to a single historical frame or trained with sliding windows that mismatch online inference and add redundant compute, degrading accuracy and training efficiency.
method: Streaming temporal attention with a BEV-level memory queue: squeeze voxel queries to BEV, align multi-frame BEV features via ego-motion compensation, and apply deformable attention over a FIFO streaming memory during both training and inference, enabling efficient multi-frame interaction with negligible latency.
paper_title: ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers

problem: Existing datasets provide only coarse object-level flow, lacking fine-grained occupancy-level motion needed to represent dynamic scenes accurately.
method: FlowOcc3D benchmark: generate per-voxel flow by rigidly mapping occupancy centers between frames using object pose transforms and computing velocities; train a joint occupancy+flow head to realize fine-grained occupancy-flow prediction and show improved dynamic scene modeling.
paper_title: ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers

problem: Common activations like ReLU/maxout have zero-gradient regions and yield non-convex training objectives, limiting stable optimization and expressivity beyond convex piecewise-linear functions; there is a gap between low-dimensional spline-based approximation and high-dimensional deep nets.
method: Introduce a lifting layer that maps a scalar x to a higher-dimensional vector via predefined labels t1<...<tL (scaled or unscaled), effectively routing x to interval-specific channels. When followed by a fully connected layer, the network realizes a linear spline, enabling universal approximation of continuous functions while allowing convex training of the last layer under convex losses; extend to vector-valued lifting via domain triangulation.
paper_title: Lifting Layers: Analysis and Applications

problem: Training with non-convex or flat (zero-gradient) loss functions (e.g., truncated linear) is difficult and sensitive to initialization, hindering robust regression under heavy outliers.
method: Lift the loss/output space: predict in the convex hull (unit simplex) of the lifted loss range and decode via the inverse lifting. This yields a tight convex relaxation with linear objective and simple simplex constraints, including closed-form solutions in simple settings, enabling robust optimization of non-convex/flat losses.
paper_title: Lifting Layers: Analysis and Applications

problem: Achieving higher accuracy and stability in vision tasks (image classification and denoising) compared to similarly sized ReLU-based CNNs.
method: Replace ReLUs with coordinate-wise scaled lifting (L=3) in CNNs, which increases channels and avoids dead activations. Demonstrate faster convergence and better generalization on CIFAR-10/100 and MNIST vs. ReLU/maxout, and a Lift-46 denoiser (16 blocks, 46 filters, lifting+BN) that attains state-of-the-art PSNR on BSD68, indicating practical gains from lifting layers.
paper_title: Lifting Layers: Analysis and Applications

problem: Lack of real-image datasets that let researchers develop and benchmark active robotic vision (next-best-view) without a physical robot, with consistent multi-view instance labels and navigable motion between views.
method: Active Vision Dataset (AVD): densely sampled RGB-D imagery (20k+ images) across 9 indoor scenes with 50k+ 2D instance boxes and movement pointers (forward/back/left/right/rotate) enabling virtual robot motion. Labels are produced by 3D instance annotation via COLMAP SfM + CMVS/PMVS dense reconstructions and projected to images, using a novel multi-view depth fusion to better handle occlusions. Scenes are scanned twice with natural object rearrangements to test generalization.
paper_title: A Dataset for Developing and Benchmarking Active Vision

problem: How to select the next camera move that improves instance classification without explicit occlusion/pose labels and while avoiding overfitting to the dataset.
method: Deep active recognition policy trained with reinforcement learning (REINFORCE): a shared CNN feature extractor (ResNet-18 conv layers) feeds an instance classifier and an action network over 6 discrete moves. The classifier is trained on BigBIRD cutouts composited onto SUN backgrounds with aggressive augmentation to generalize to small-scale, cluttered views; the action network is rewarded by classification correctness/confidence. The learned policy reliably increases accuracy with more moves and outperforms random and always-forward baselines.
paper_title: A Dataset for Developing and Benchmarking Active Vision

problem: Weighted subsampling aims to mimic the full-set ERM, suffers high-variance from importance weights, requires training changes, and cannot theoretically outperform the full-set model.
method: Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS): select training samples without reweighting by using influence functions computed on a validation/test distribution; prove that making per-sample perturbations negatively correlated with their influence guarantees the subset model can outperform the full-set model, and map perturbations in [-1/n,0] to sampling probabilities to implement unweighted selection.
paper_title: Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function

problem: Deterministic influence-based dropout can be overconfident to a specific validation distribution and fail under distribution shift (poor out-of-sample generalization).
method: Design probabilistic sampling functions (linear and sigmoid in the sample influence) with tunable gradient bound σ; analyze via distributionally robust optimization over a χ²-divergence ball that worst-case risk is Lipschitz in the influence vector with constant O(σ√((2δ+1)/n)), enabling control of robustness; introduce a surrogate confidence metric Γ=||θ~−θ^||² with matching Lipschitz order to guide hyperparameter selection.
paper_title: Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function

problem: Influence function computation is expensive and ill-conditioned in high-dimensional sparse settings.
method: Efficiently compute inverse Hessian–vector products using a Hessian-free mixed preconditioned conjugate gradient (PCG) method (mixing identity and Hessian-diagonal preconditioners), enabling scalable IF-based subsampling on large sparse datasets.
paper_title: Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function

problem: Per-frame object detectors in videos degrade under motion blur/defocus/rare poses, and naive multi-frame averaging hurts due to temporal misalignment.
method: Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation (FGFA): for each reference frame, estimate optical flow (FlowNet) to neighboring frames, bilinearly warp their feature maps into the reference frame, and aggregate them with spatially varying adaptive weights computed as cosine similarity in a learned embedding space; feed the aggregated feature to an R-FCN detector. All modules (feature extractor, flow, embedding, detector) are trained end-to-end, yielding large gains, especially for fast motion.
paper_title: Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation for Video Object Detection

problem: Training with long temporal windows is memory-intensive, and prior video detection pipelines rely on fixed flow and post-hoc box linking rather than joint learning.
method: A fully differentiable architecture with bilinear warping enables end-to-end joint training, coupled with temporal dropout: randomly sample neighbors from a large temporal range while training with a small window, enabling robust large-window aggregation at inference. A sliding feature-buffer inference algorithm and an optional flow-composition variant reduce compute with only minor accuracy loss, and joint training outperforms using frozen flow/detector modules.
paper_title: Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation for Video Object Detection

problem: Aerial target tracking in cluttered environments requires maintaining safety, visibility (occlusion avoidance, limited FOV), and smoothness, but existing planners lack flexibility and often fail under abrupt target motions and infeasible initial states.
method: A hierarchical multi-goal, occlusion-aware path search over predicted target waypoints defines occlusion-free regions and selects visible seeds with a greedy A*; a smart safe flight corridor (SFC) generation strategy then builds polytopes aligned with the drone’s initial velocity and path intersections, reducing overlap and handling infeasible starts to provide a robust topological guide.
paper_title: Elastic Tracker: A Spatio-temporal Trajectory Planner for Flexible Aerial Tracking

problem: Visibility handling via ESDF-based metrics is computationally heavy and brittle as hard constraints in cluttered scenes.
method: An analytical occlusion/visibility cost is derived without ESDF by leveraging the occlusion-aware path structure and sector-shaped visible regions; combined with a tailored distance-keeping cost that treats horizontal/vertical components asymmetrically (C2 penalty rising sharply when too close and linearly when far), yielding adaptive, efficient visibility and distance maintenance.
paper_title: Elastic Tracker: A Spatio-temporal Trajectory Planner for Flexible Aerial Tracking

problem: Generating a spatiotemporal trajectory that jointly satisfies dynamic feasibility, collision avoidance, visibility, proper distance, and time allocation is computationally challenging and prone to discontinuities.
method: A T-MINCO-based joint optimization with two penalty schemes: relative-time integral penalties for dynamics and collision inside the SFC, and absolute-time penalties at target prediction times for visibility and distance; continuity is preserved via MINCO’s q,T mapping despite changing piece assignments, and a time-slack constraint is eliminated via a smooth variable transform—enabling fast, robust spatiotemporal trajectory optimization with guaranteed safety and visibility.
paper_title: Elastic Tracker: A Spatio-temporal Trajectory Planner for Flexible Aerial Tracking

problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation with only image-level labels relies on sparse, coarse CAMs and fixed off-the-shelf saliency maps, yielding poor object coverage and boundaries.
method: AuxSegNet: a joint multi-task framework with a shared backbone and three branches for multi-label image classification, saliency detection, and semantic segmentation. Saliency detection and classification act as auxiliary tasks; off-the-shelf saliency maps serve as initial pseudo labels that are updated online, enabling feature sharing that enhances foreground/background separation and improves segmentation without extra pixel-level annotations.
paper_title: Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation

problem: Existing pseudo-label generation underuses the correlation between saliency and segmentation and lacks a principled way to densify CAMs and sharpen boundaries.
method: Cross-task global pixel affinity learning: learn task-specific non-local affinity maps from saliency and segmentation features, fuse them via a learned self-attention into a cross-task affinity matrix, and impose multi-task constraints by refining both branches’ predictions. Use the learned affinity to refine saliency outputs and to propagate/refine CAM activations to generate improved pseudo labels, iterated stage-wise to progressively boost segmentation performance.
paper_title: Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation

problem: Contact-rich manipulation often fails when planners produce only kinematic targets; robots need to infer both where to move and how much force to apply from vision and language in unseen environments and with a moving camera.
method: ForceSight, a text-conditioned RGBD Vision Transformer (ViT-L pre-trained on ImageNet21k) with early-fused depth and cross-attention to a frozen T5 encoder at every block, predicts a visual-force goal in the current camera frame: an affordance + masked-depth map to select a 3D end-effector target (x,y,z), plus yaw, gripper width, applied force vector, and grip force scalar.
paper_title: ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals

problem: Executing predicted goals robustly without precise calibration is difficult; vision-only servoing underperforms on tasks requiring force modulation and contact reasoning.
method: An eye-in-hand visual-force servoing controller that jointly minimizes kinematic and force errors in the camera frame to generate incremental end-effector and gripper motions; action primitives (e.g., approach, grasp, pull) are appended to the prompt and sequenced via a simple state machine to chain keyframe-like goals across multi-step tasks, yielding strong real-world performance and large drops when force goals are removed.
paper_title: ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals

problem: Obtaining scalable supervision for text-conditioned, force-aware mobile manipulation is costly, especially for both kinematic and force labels.
method: A 30-hour teleoperation pipeline producing 26k datapoints by mapping multiple RGBD views to the same goal, using ArUco + kinematics for fingertip targets, wrist F/T sensing for applied forces, and an MLP trained from motor telemetry to estimate grip force; robustness enhanced via data augmentations and sampling recovery states.
paper_title: ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals

problem: Online video instance segmentation struggles to model frame-to-frame temporal context: pixel-only fusion loses instance semantics, while ROI-cropping–based instance fusion depends on detection quality and discards global context.
method: Introduce a hybrid, box-free representation per frame with a global, order-aware instance code (latent slots) plus CNN feature maps, and perform cropping-free temporal fusion via hybrid attentions—code-to-code, code-to-pixel, and pixel-to-code—both intra-frame and inter-frame. Transformer layers are inserted into the CNN backbone and attend across multiple reference frames with positional encodings, enabling instance-aware temporal aggregation without RoI cropping or heavy decoders.
paper_title: Hybrid Instance-aware Temporal Fusion for Online Video Instance Segmentation

problem: Maintaining consistent instance identities online typically requires fragile, heuristic cross-frame matching.
method: Make instance identity matching ‘free’ by enforcing order/slot consistency of the global instance codes across frames. A training objective jointly assigns predictions over current and previous frames, adding order constraints so the same slot persists over time; at inference, identities are read directly from slot indices (with an IoU fallback), eliminating explicit per-frame association.
paper_title: Hybrid Instance-aware Temporal Fusion for Online Video Instance Segmentation

problem: Global LiDAR–camera fusion often misaligns modalities by using coarse voxel centers and aggregates image features over the whole scene, yielding suboptimal cross-modal alignment.
method: Centroid Dynamic Fusion (CDF): represent each non-empty voxel by its point centroid, project centroids to images, and fuse voxel/image features with deformable cross-attention to sample around learned dynamic offsets, improving precise global alignment; followed by ROI pooling for proposal features.
paper_title: LoGoNet: Towards Accurate 3D Object Detection with Local-to-Global Cross-Modal Fusion

problem: Global fusion lacks fine-grained, region-level cues needed to disambiguate small/distant objects within proposals.
method: Local Fusion (LoF) with Position Information Encoder (PIE) and Grid Dynamic Fusion (GDF): uniformly grid each proposal, encode local raw-point geometry (relative grid position, point stats/centroids), project grid centers to images to sample nearby image features, and fuse via cross-attention to form locally fused ROI-grid features.
paper_title: LoGoNet: Towards Accurate 3D Object Detection with Local-to-Global Cross-Modal Fusion

problem: Globally and locally fused features are produced independently with limited interaction, leaving complementary cues underutilized.
method: Feature Dynamic Aggregation (FDA): apply transformer-based self-attention over encoded and fused grid features (FpB, FlB, FgB) with residual connections to dynamically integrate local–global information for stronger second-stage refinement.
paper_title: LoGoNet: Towards Accurate 3D Object Detection with Local-to-Global Cross-Modal Fusion

problem: Incorporating temporal information for multi-view 3D detection in PETR-like models where explicit BEV feature alignment is not feasible.
method: Temporal modeling via 3D Coordinates Alignment: transform previous-frame 3D coordinates into the current frame using ego-motion/pose (with global coordinates as a bridge), generate aligned 3D position embeddings for both frames, concatenate features and feed as keys/values to the transformer decoder—achieving temporal alignment and improved velocity estimation without explicit feature warping.
paper_title: PETRv2: A Unified Framework for 3D Perception from Multi-Camera Images

problem: Data-independent 3D position embeddings in PETR limit adaptability and robustness, failing to exploit image-driven cues (e.g., depth).
method: Feature-Guided Position Encoder (FPE): derive attention weights from projected 2D features via 1×1 conv + MLP + sigmoid to reweight MLP-encoded 3D coordinates, producing data-dependent 3D PEs; use PE+2D features as keys and 2D features as values—improving accuracy and robustness to extrinsic noise.
paper_title: PETRv2: A Unified Framework for 3D Perception from Multi-Camera Images

problem: Scaling DETR-style 3D perception to multiple tasks (BEV segmentation, 3D lane detection) without excessive dense BEV queries.
method: Unified sparse-query multitask design: introduce task-specific queries initialized in different spaces—3D detection queries in world space, segmentation queries as BEV patch anchors, and lane queries as 3D anchor lanes (uniform Y-axis sampling, parallel to Y)—all processed by a shared transformer decoder with task-specific heads, yielding efficient SOTA results across tasks.
paper_title: PETRv2: A Unified Framework for 3D Perception from Multi-Camera Images

problem: Monocular SLAM produces trajectories and maps with unknown scale and time-varying scale drift, yet many applications require online metric scale without extra sensors.
method: An online Bayesian filter (discrete Bayes or Kalman) that estimates a per-frame scale correction by fusing generic object detections with class-specific height priors and the current SLAM local map, then applies the estimated scale to correct the trajectory and map in real time.
paper_title: Bayesian Scale Estimation for Monocular SLAM Based on Generic Object Detection for Correcting Scale Drift

problem: 2D object detections and noisy 3D map points give unreliable scale cues due to occlusions/background points; a robust measurement model is needed to turn each detection into a scale observation with uncertainty.
method: A probabilistic observation model that: (i) collects local map points projecting inside the detection box, (ii) uses gamma-weighted depth ranking to select a representative surface point, (iii) assumes known vertical direction to build a vertical 3D line through this point, (iv) intersects back-projected rays of the box’s top/bottom with this line to estimate object height, and (v) derives a scale observation and its variance via uncertainty propagation.
paper_title: Bayesian Scale Estimation for Monocular SLAM Based on Generic Object Detection for Correcting Scale Drift

problem: Scale drift evolves over time and depends on camera motion; without a dynamics model, scale estimates are less stable and less adaptive.
method: A motion model for the scale correction as a Gaussian random walk with process noise proportional to accumulated camera rotation since the last update, bounded between s_min and s_max, which adaptively captures drift severity for improved filtering.
paper_title: Bayesian Scale Estimation for Monocular SLAM Based on Generic Object Detection for Correcting Scale Drift

problem: Transferring skills from a privileged-state teacher to a visual sensorimotor policy typically locks the student into the teacher’s behavior and fails to adapt to perception-induced differences (e.g., occlusions), while pure visual RL is sample-inefficient.
method: Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient (TAPG): a two-stage framework where a teacher is first trained via RL on low-dimensional object pose (3D bbox), then a student is trained on segmented point clouds using a combined policy-gradient objective plus a gated behavior-cloning loss that imitates only when the teacher’s advantage is higher, yielding adaptation to the visual observation space with performance guarantees.
paper_title: Grasp Anything: Combining Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient Learning with Instance Segmentation to Grasp Arbitrary Objects

problem: Segmentation/tracking can fail when the robot’s hand occludes the target during grasping, causing visual policies distilled from teachers to break in practice.
method: Perception-driven reward shaping that adds a target-visibility term (visibility ratio computed by projecting sampled surface points and checking mask overlap) to encourage grasps that maintain object visibility, leading to perception-aware strategies (e.g., finger retraction, angled grasps).
paper_title: Grasp Anything: Combining Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient Learning with Instance Segmentation to Grasp Arbitrary Objects

problem: Grasping arbitrary user-specified objects in clutter and transferring from simulation to the real robot without finetuning requires robust target selection and observation matching between sim and real.
method: Instance-segmentation-guided control: represent the target as a segmented point cloud (GT masks in sim; SAM-based promptable segmentation at deployment) and perform zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, aided by tendon-hand dynamics calibration via ArUco-based system identification.
paper_title: Grasp Anything: Combining Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient Learning with Instance Segmentation to Grasp Arbitrary Objects

problem: Real-world vision-based robotic RL is sample-inefficient and costly due to reliance on human demonstrations and sparse rewards.
method: Bootstrap the critic using large-scale simulated rollouts generated by a simulation-pretrained visuomotor policy, complemented with a small number of real-world rollouts from the same policy to regularize Q and avoid sim-real value mismatch; integrated into RLPD with balanced sampling (25% sim demos, 25% real demos, 50% replay), and optionally using hybrid simulated demos to expand state coverage.
paper_title: SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training

problem: Online exploration in multi-stage, contact-rich tasks is slow and brittle when relying solely on the learned RL policy.
method: Leverage the simulation-pretrained policy for action proposals during real-world training, selecting between the RL action and the pretrained (BC) action via a Boltzmann distribution over ensemble critic Q-values; reuse the selected proposal for value targets, improving exploration and learning stability without human intervention.
paper_title: SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training

problem: The visual sim-to-real gap limits transfer of sim-pretrained vision policies for real-world control.
method: Build a task-specific digital twin using 3D Gaussian Splatting for photorealistic rendering with Isaac Gym/MuJoCo physics, pre-train a visuomotor policy by distilling a privileged state-based RL policy (RL→BC), and mitigate the gap via camera pose randomization, strong image augmentations, and background masking with SAM2 tracking for real-time segmentation.
paper_title: SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training

problem: Pointwise LLM rerankers trained as binary classifiers produce overconfident yes/no outputs (especially with CoT), yielding poor score discrimination and weak ranking quality.
method: Reframe reranking as a generative task with fine-grained integer outputs (0–10). Train via SFT to generate CoT plus an integer score, and compute the final score as s_i × P(token=s_i), leveraging autoregressive probabilities for more discriminative ranking.
paper_title: ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking

problem: Pointwise rerankers lack global ranking awareness, while listwise RL methods that provide it are latency- and memory-heavy at inference.
method: Apply GRPO-based RL to the SFT model with a novel listwise-derived reward that aggregates N×G rollout scores across all candidates to assign reciprocal-rank rewards to positives, penalize negatives above positives, and stabilize with SFT reference scores and a KL penalty—instilling listwise ordering signals while keeping pointwise inference.
paper_title: ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking

problem: High-quality, fine-grained labels for reasoning-intensive reranking are scarce, limiting effective SFT for nuanced relevance.
method: Synthesize training data using a reasoning teacher LLM to generate CoT and integer scores; use multi-sample consensus to denoise/stabilize scores, and construct diverse candidate sets with hard/medium/easy negatives—yielding a robust SFT corpus for nuanced relevance assessment.
paper_title: ERANK: Fusing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Effective and Efficient Text Reranking

problem: Non-optical tactile sensors typically trade off sensitivity, dynamic range, and ease of integration, limiting reliable force sensing across delicate-to-heavy contacts for manipulation.
method: PhysTac: a compact MEMS-based pressure taxel array (8 CPS135B-1500D units) embedded under an Ecoflex elastomer with a 3D-printed housing, achieving millinewton-level resolution and >200 N range; spatial pressure maps reconstructed via RBF interpolation, delivering a much larger dynamic range than prior MEMS tactile skins.
paper_title: TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model

problem: Do step-level reward models for mathematical reasoning actually need natural language thought descriptions to be effective?
method: Decompose each reasoning step into natural language thoughts and mathematical expressions; use MCTS to auto-generate step-level preference pairs; train and compare Full-Context SRM (thoughts+equations) vs Math-Only SRM (equations only) as scoring functions in greedy/beam search on GSM8K and MATH. Finding: removing natural language has negligible impact; math-only inputs suffice and sometimes outperform.
paper_title: What Are Step-Level Reward Models Rewarding? Counterintuitive Findings from MCTS-boosted Mathematical Reasoning

problem: Lack of accurate, practical simulators for non-visuotactile sensors hinders simulation-driven policy/data generation due to a sim-to-real gap in tactile responses.
method: DigiTac: a digital twin trained by pairing FEM soft-body simulations (Isaac Gym) of the elastomer with real sensor readings; synchronize via DTW on force, then learn a sim-to-real mapping from von Mises stress at a sparse set of surface nodes above each taxel (aggregated features) to taxel outputs using a lightweight MLP with noise-based domain randomization, achieving ~6% error relative to max pressure.
paper_title: TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model

problem: Collecting large real-world tactile datasets is slow and intrusive, causing poor performance when training with limited data.
method: Use DigiTac to synthesize diverse indentation sequences (varying depth/speed/angle) to augment small real datasets for a time-series Transformer indenter-shape classifier; hybrid training boosts accuracy from ~33.6% (real-only) to ~95% (sim+real), demonstrating effective Sim2Real data augmentation.
paper_title: TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model

problem: Are SRMs merely rewarding single-step arithmetic correctness, or can they evaluate multi-step logical coherence in mathematical language?
method: Contrast Math-Only SRM (consumes full history of equations) with Single-Step Math-Only SRM (only latest equation) using identical MCTS-derived preferences; measure downstream gains. Finding: substantial performance gap in favor of MO-SRM indicates SRMs capture cross-step logical coherence in math expressions.
paper_title: What Are Step-Level Reward Models Rewarding? Counterintuitive Findings from MCTS-boosted Mathematical Reasoning

problem: Can SRMs reliably evaluate the logical quality of next-step natural language thoughts?
method: Train a Next-Thought SRM that scores proposed next thoughts given prior context (without equations), using the same MCTS preference signals; assess training preference accuracy and downstream greedy search impact. Finding: NT-SRM trains poorly and yields little/no gains, showing SRMs struggle to assess coherence from natural language descriptions alone.
paper_title: What Are Step-Level Reward Models Rewarding? Counterintuitive Findings from MCTS-boosted Mathematical Reasoning

problem: Adding heavy-ball momentum to Gradient TD methods (GTD, GTD2, TDC) lacks convergence/stability guarantees; naive momentum in SA can diverge and parameter choices are unclear.
method: Introduce heavy-ball momentum into Gradient TD and set a vanishing-to-one momentum schedule eta_t = rho_t - w * alpha_t / rho_{t-1} with rho_t -> 0. Algebraically decompose the momentum updates into three coupled recursions (v, u, theta) with distinct step sizes, and prove almost-sure convergence to the TD solution using the ODE method in two regimes: (i) one-timescale by showing the driving matrix G is Hurwitz via a Routh–Hurwitz argument under w(w+1) > ||Ā||^2; (ii) three-timescale with milder requirement w > 0, leveraging new SA theory.
paper_title: Gradient Temporal Difference with Momentum: Stability and Convergence

problem: There is no general theory ensuring stability and convergence for three-timescale stochastic approximation, limiting analysis of algorithms with nested estimators.
method: Develop the first general stability and almost-sure convergence conditions for three-timescale SA. Specify Lipschitz, martingale-noise, step-size separation, and limiting-ODE assumptions (B1–B7), and use scaling plus ODE-tracking arguments to prove boundedness and convergence of (x_n, y_n, z_n) to their equilibria.
paper_title: Gradient Temporal Difference with Momentum: Stability and Convergence

problem: The practical effect of heavy-ball momentum on policy-evaluation with Gradient TD is unknown.
method: Instantiate GTD-M, GTD2-M, and TDC-M with the proposed momentum schedule and step-size choices (one-TS and three-TS), and evaluate on Boyan Chain, 5-/19-state random walks, and random MDP. Show consistent RMSPBE improvements and faster early-phase learning, particularly in the three-timescale setting that permits larger initial momentum.
paper_title: Gradient Temporal Difference with Momentum: Stability and Convergence

problem: Large human motion datasets contain many movements that are dynamically infeasible for a real humanoid, and naive retargeting harms training of whole-body imitators.
method: A scalable 'sim-to-data' pipeline: (1) fit SMPL shape to the humanoid (β′) and retarget via IK on 12 corresponding joints to preserve end-effector positions; (2) train a privileged full-state motion imitator on all retargeted sequences and automatically filter out sequences it cannot track, yielding a large humanoid-feasible motion set.
paper_title: Learning Human-to-Humanoid Real-Time Whole-Body Teleoperation

problem: Enabling precise real-time whole-body teleoperation (including lower body) from only an RGB camera without markers or force sensors requires a goal representation aligned with deployable sensing.
method: Train a goal-conditioned RL policy whose observation uses only deployable signals: proprioception plus robot-centric 3D keypoint goals (positions, velocities, and robot–reference offsets for 8 keypoints). Use dense full-body imitation rewards and PD joint targets. At inference, drive goals with a single-camera 3D pose estimator (HybrIK) for real-time tracking.
paper_title: Learning Human-to-Humanoid Real-Time Whole-Body Teleoperation

problem: Sim-to-real gaps and instability hinder zero-shot deployment of learned whole-body controllers on full-sized humanoids across diverse motions.
method: Sim-to-real training with extensive dynamics and actuation randomization (friction, masses, CoM, PD gains, torque noise, control delays), external pushes, varied terrains, plus safety/regularization rewards and early termination. The resulting controller transfers zero-shot to a Unitree H1 and robustly executes dynamic tasks (walking, kicking, jumping, pushing, pick-and-place).
paper_title: Learning Human-to-Humanoid Real-Time Whole-Body Teleoperation

problem: Offline policy learning for large-catalog recommender systems requires gradients that sum over all actions; computing the softmax normalizer and expectation makes each SGD step O(P).
method: Derive a covariance-form policy gradient that eliminates the partition function: ∇θ E_{a∼πθ}[r̂(a,x)] = Cov_{a∼πθ}(r̂(a,x), ∇θ fθ(a,x)). This enables Monte Carlo estimation of the gradient without computing Zθ(x).
paper_title: Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation

problem: Sampling from the target policy πθ to estimate expectations still costs O(P) and can be high variance.
method: Use self-normalized importance sampling (SNIS) to approximate expectations under πθ with a small biased but efficient estimator, avoiding exact sampling from πθ and reducing variance via weighted averaging.
paper_title: Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation

problem: Designing a proposal distribution for SNIS that is both fast and close to πθ over millions of items.
method: Fix item embeddings β and learn only the user transform hθ; build an approximate MIPS index on β and define a mixture proposal q_{ε,K} combining uniform sampling and Top-K actions retrieved by MIPS using current scores hθ(x)^Tβ. This yields O(log P) sampling/evaluation and maintains closeness to πθ, delivering order-of-magnitude training speedups without degrading policy quality.
paper_title: Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation

problem: Unsupervised domain adaptation typically assumes a single or discretely labeled target domain, which breaks down when real-world targets are unlabeled mixtures of multiple domains (and vary continuously). How to adapt from a labeled source to such compound targets without domain labels?
method: Instance-specific curriculum domain adaptation: disentangle class-discriminative vs. domain-specific factors via a class-confusion adversarial objective (with reconstruction for completeness and random-label confusion for orthogonality) to obtain domain features; rank target instances by their domain-gap to the source in this space; progressively adapt from easy (near-source) to hard (far) target samples while maintaining class discrimination and adversarial domain confusion.
paper_title: Open Compound Domain Adaptation

problem: Models overfit seen domains and fail on previously unseen (open) domains at inference. How to make predictions robust when test inputs come from novel domains?
method: Memory-augmented representation: maintain a class prototype memory built from source data; compute a domain indicator from the learned domain encoder to gauge domain gap; adaptively combine the input’s direct feature with a memory-enhanced feature (softmax-weighted prototypes) using the domain indicator, with cosine normalization, yielding agile, domain-aware predictions on open domains.
paper_title: Open Compound Domain Adaptation

problem: Understand how modern detectors behave for instance-level recognition across view, scale, and occlusion in real scenes, and determine a sampling resolution that faithfully simulates continuous robot motion.
method: Adapt SSD (VGG backbone) for fast instance detection on object instances and analyze performance over densely sampled viewpoints to quantify degradation from scale, occlusion, and viewing direction; use real robot videos to measure detection-score change vs. camera displacement, validating that 30 cm sampling closely matches smaller steps for recognition behavior.
paper_title: A Dataset for Developing and Benchmarking Active Vision

problem: End-to-end RL for LLM agents jointly optimizes planning and summarization, causing competing objectives and hard credit assignment that impede improving planning quality.
method: RLTR decouples training by optimizing only the Planner with a single-objective, multi-turn RL loop (PPO/GRPO/REINFORCE++ with KL regularization to a reference), using action-focused templates that terminate before answer generation and mask non-action losses, yielding a modular Planner that can pair with any Summarizer.
paper_title: Encouraging Good Processes Without the Need for Good Answers: Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agent Planning

problem: Industrial data rarely has verifiable final answers, and reward models on answers are vulnerable to reward hacking, producing unreliable RL signals.
method: Introduce a tool-use completeness reward: an LLM-based checker evaluates whether the action sequence covers all necessary tool calls (γ), averaged over N samples, combined with format validation and rule-based penalties for repetition and incorrect tool use. This directly rewards planning quality without needing verifiable answers and correlates better with human labels.
paper_title: Encouraging Good Processes Without the Need for Good Answers: Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agent Planning

problem: Planner-only RL is unstable to start from scratch due to lack of high-quality planning trajectories.
method: Cold-start the Planner via knowledge distillation from a strong teacher LLM: sample multiple action trajectories, apply best-of-n rejection sampling to curate high-quality plans, and perform SFT on questions-to-actions before RL, stabilizing and accelerating subsequent optimization.
paper_title: Encouraging Good Processes Without the Need for Good Answers: Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agent Planning

problem: RL for long-horizon, sparse-reward manipulation suffers from ineffective action-level exploration, while policies built directly from options can be suboptimal and inflexible.
method: Perform look-ahead tree search during exploration over a small set of learned multi-goal skills, using their coarse transition models and success probabilities to choose purposeful skill-goal executions; train the final task policy over primitive actions with HER+DDPG, decoupling temporal abstraction for exploration from fine-grained policy representation.
paper_title: Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control

problem: Step-wise learned world models from random exploration have poor coverage and accumulate error when unrolled, limiting their usefulness for planning in continuous control.
method: First learn generalized manipulation skills (reach, grasp, transfer) via multi-goal RL (HER+DDPG), then fit for each skill a coarse transition regressor T_i(s, g) -> s_final and a success predictor u_i(s, g). Planning over these temporally extended, purposefully collected models reduces error accumulation and targets useful regions of state space.
paper_title: Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control

problem: Exploration strategies often depend heavily on a specific option set or exact environment dynamics, hurting robustness and sample efficiency.
method: Use goal-parameterized skill dynamics with probability-based pruning in a bounded-branch tree search, enabling effective exploration even with redundant/missing skills and moderate dynamics mismatch; empirically outperforms HER, parameterized-action hierarchical baselines, and parameter-space noise in complex manipulation tasks.
paper_title: Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control

problem: Continuous-action RL requires approximate maximization over actions or naive discretization that explodes with dimension; behavioral cloning collapses multimodal human demonstrations to a single action.
method: AQuaDem learns a state-conditioned discretization Ψ(s) that outputs K candidate continuous actions via a multi-head network trained with a soft-min (temperature-controlled) reconstruction loss. This captures multimodal demo behavior and converts the continuous MDP into a small, state-dependent discrete action set enabling exact argmax with standard discrete RL. The loss connects to GMMs and admits T→0/∞ limits (mode selection vs averaging).
paper_title: Continuous Control with Action Quantization from Demonstrations

problem: Sample-efficient RL with sparse rewards from limited human demonstrations is hard due to poor exploration and approximate policy improvement in continuous control.
method: AQuaDQN: a two-stage pipeline where (1) Ψ(s) is learned offline from demos, then (2) a discrete Q-learner (Munchausen DQN) acts over the K learned candidates with exact argmax, using dual replay buffers (env + demos, demo actions mapped to nearest candidate). This filters implausible actions and leverages demo priors for exploration and stability.
paper_title: Continuous Control with Action Quantization from Demonstrations

problem: Imitation Learning from human demos and RL from unlabeled play data struggle with multimodality and absent rewards/task labels; continuous-action baselines (e.g., SAC/GAIL) underutilize these priors.
method: Apply AQuaDem’s discretization as a backbone for downstream learners: (a) AQuaGAIL—discrete GAIL where the policy selects among Ψ(s)’s candidates while a discriminator supplies rewards; (b) AQuaPlay—learn Ψ(s) from unlabeled play offline, then train per-task discrete RL on top. The state-dependent candidate sets encode human/play priors without curse-of-dimensionality from uniform grids.
paper_title: Continuous Control with Action Quantization from Demonstrations

problem: Unsupervised sentence summarization models are mostly extractive or rely on word-level reconstruction, yielding limited abstraction and poor control over target length.
method: Formulate abstractive summarization as an RL problem with a self-critical policy gradient on a seq2seq policy (T5). Design a composite reward: (1) semantic content preservation via sentence-embedding cosine similarity (sent2vec/SBERT), (2) fluency via LM perplexity (fine-tuned GPT-2 with shaped reward), and (3) explicit length control using a prepended length prompt and a length-deviation reward; decode by maximizing these rewards.
paper_title: Generating Multiple-Length Summaries via Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Sentence Summarization

problem: Summary quality varies by target length (shorter is harder); training separate models or lengths independently fails to transfer strengths across lengths.
method: Introduce multi-summary learning (MSL): for each input, generate summaries at multiple target lengths and add a cross-summary reward that encourages a target summary to be semantically close to higher-quality summaries of nearby lengths. Use a usefulness function based on quality difference and length proximity, enabling summaries of different lengths to mutually enhance each other during training without increasing inference-time complexity.
paper_title: Generating Multiple-Length Summaries via Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Sentence Summarization

problem: RL training from scratch is unstable and lacks built-in length control; models need an initialization that teaches summarization operations and controllable length without references.
method: Pretrain with prompt-based text reconstruction (PTR): perturb texts by shuffling, dropping, and adding words; prepend a length prompt indicating the original length; train the model to reconstruct the original text. This imparts reordering, deletion/addition skills and explicit length control, improving RL convergence and final performance (stronger than generic denoising pretraining like BART for this task).
paper_title: Generating Multiple-Length Summaries via Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Sentence Summarization

problem: Safe RL must satisfy instantaneous hard constraints at every step when both states and actions can be unsafe, while learning efficiently in episodic linear mixture MDPs.
method: Proposes LSVI-NEW, which constructs safe subgraphs via backward lookahead: at each step it estimates safe state and action sets that (1) satisfy the per-step constraint for all possible next states and (2) only transition to states estimated safe at the next step. It builds a high-confidence safety estimator by projecting features onto the span of a seed-safe transition and its orthogonal complement with regularized least squares and a UCB bonus, guaranteeing step-wise safety with high probability.
paper_title: A NEAR-OPTIMAL ALGORITHM FOR SAFE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING UNDER INSTANTANEOUS HARD CONSTRAINTS

problem: Safety restricts exploration and couples decisions across time, creating an optimism–pessimism dilemma that can degrade regret.
method: Augments Q-value estimates with three targeted exploration bonuses that "look ahead and peek backward": (i) a max-over-next-state transition UCB to prioritize uncertain transitions, (ii) a future-subgraph UCB to reduce downstream uncertainty that blocks optimal current actions, and (iii) a past-subgraph UCB to address upstream uncertainty affecting reachable sets. These bonuses are parameterized to reflect remaining safety margins across steps, yielding near-optimal regret ~O(d H^3 sqrt(dK) / Δ_c) while remaining safe.
paper_title: A NEAR-OPTIMAL ALGORITHM FOR SAFE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING UNDER INSTANTANEOUS HARD CONSTRAINTS

problem: Standard value-function optimism invariants fail under non-ergodic safe subgraphs, complicating regret guarantees and necessity results.
method: Introduces a new analysis framework: constructs specialized value functions over safe and near-optimal state/action sets, proves novel invariants despite differing state supports, and quantifies future and past step impacts to bound regret. Establishes high-probability step-wise safety and a lower bound ~Ω(max{d H √K, H/Δ_c^2}), proving the dependence on the safety gap Δ_c is necessary and nearly matching the upper bound.
paper_title: A NEAR-OPTIMAL ALGORITHM FOR SAFE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING UNDER INSTANTANEOUS HARD CONSTRAINTS

problem: LLM-based theorem provers waste search budget on syntactically invalid or inapplicable tactics, and existing RL/PRM approaches rely on costly trajectory-level rollouts with sparse rewards.
method: LeanListener: a verifier-in-the-loop, step-wise fine-tuning framework that uses Lean’s immediate feedback to assign local rewards—penalizing invalid tactics and rewarding applicable ones proportionally to the reduction in remaining subgoals via a softplus(delta-goals) signal—optimized with critic-free GRPO over groups of beam-sampled tactics, requiring only single-step lookahead rather than full trajectories.
paper_title: Local Look-Ahead Guidance via Verifier-in-the-Loop for Automated Theorem Proving

problem: Supervised imitation and offline DPO on static valid/invalid pairs fail to prioritize the most effective valid tactics, cannot discover alternative tactics, and mismatch inference-time beam-search exploration.
method: On-policy online training that periodically updates the sampling model and, at each proof state, beam-samples multiple tactics (including gold), validates each with Lean, and applies normalized group rewards in GRPO. This brings proof-tree expansion into training, auto-augments with newly discovered applicable tactics, and re-ranks probability mass toward high-efficacy steps—outperforming offline/DPO in valid top-k tactics, Pass@1, proof length, and inference time.
paper_title: Local Look-Ahead Guidance via Verifier-in-the-Loop for Automated Theorem Proving

problem: Implicit-feedback recommenders trained with classic point-wise/pair-wise losses learn popularity-biased rankings because model optimization mixes true user-item relevance with exposure propensity; IPS-based debiasing needs hard-to-estimate propensities, causing high variance and residual bias.
method: Provide a theoretical analysis showing classic objectives embed user/item propensity factors into the learned scores. Propose Fair Sampling (FS): for every observed sample, construct complementary samples so each user and each item are equally likely to appear as positive and as negative. This balances selection at the sampling level and analytically cancels propensity terms in the gradients, yielding unbiased preference learning without estimating propensity scores.
paper_title: Unbiased Collaborative Filtering with Fair Sampling

problem: A practical, model-agnostic way is needed to remove popularity bias across common training regimes (point-wise and pair-wise) without changing model architectures.
method: Instantiate FS-Point and FS-Pair that augment training sets: FS-Point pairs each (u,i; y) with (ũ,ĩ; y), (ũ,i; 1−y), (u,ĩ; 1−y); FS-Pair complements each (u, i+, j−) with (ũ, j+, i−). These plug-in samplers for cross-entropy and BPR provably stop optimizing propensity factors and achieve state-of-the-art Recall/NDCG with lower ARP on Kindle, Gowalla, and Yelp.
paper_title: Unbiased Collaborative Filtering with Fair Sampling

problem: Conversational recommenders for complex e-commerce products overlook users’ lack of domain knowledge and struggle with underspecified goals, leading to poor recommendations and shallow dialogs.
method: SalesOps: a knowledge-grounded CRS framework where the Seller is equipped with a buying guide and curated product catalog, while the Shopper’s preferences are revealed gradually via LLM-based detection of seller questions; includes a multi-dimensional evaluation of recommendation accuracy, educational informativeness (NLI/quiz), and fluency/professionalism.
paper_title: Salespeople vs SalesBot: Exploring the Role of Educational Value in Conversational Recommender Systems

problem: There is no scalable, reproducible way to simulate realistic seller–shopper interactions for CRS that educate and recommend.
method: Two LLM-powered agents: SalesBot (modular tool-use pipeline with LLM action decision, FAISS retrieval over buying guides, embedding-based product search, and LLM response generation with regeneration) and ShopperBot (preference-conditioned dialogue with controlled [ACCEPT]/[REJECT] and semantic-triggered gradual preference revelation), enabling human–human, human–bot, bot–human, and bot–bot evaluations.
paper_title: Salespeople vs SalesBot: Exploring the Role of Educational Value in Conversational Recommender Systems

problem: Unclear which LLM components most impact CRS quality, and how LLM sellers compare to professionals; ensuring faithfulness in sales dialogs is challenging.
method: Ablations replacing LLM modules with rules/keywords/smaller models to quantify effects via GPT-4-based metrics; a human study with 15 professional salespeople vs SalesBot on recommendation quality, informativeness, and fluency; GPT-4/NLI-based faithfulness analysis revealing both LLM hallucinations and human upselling/unbacked claims.
paper_title: Salespeople vs SalesBot: Exploring the Role of Educational Value in Conversational Recommender Systems

problem: Sequential recommenders in the matching stage usually compress a user's behavior history into a single embedding, which cannot represent multiple concurrent interests and weakly captures correlations across adjacent actions.
method: ComiRec learns K interest embeddings per user from behavior sequences. It offers two instantiations: (1) ComiRec-DR applies CapsNet-style dynamic routing from primary (item) capsules to interest capsules on ordered behaviors to cluster actions into distinct interests while preserving sequential information; (2) ComiRec-SA uses multi-head self-attention with positional encodings to attend to different interest facets. Training assigns each target item to its nearest interest embedding (argmax) with sampled-softmax; serving retrieves top-N per-interest via ANN (e.g., Faiss).
paper_title: Controllable Multi-Interest Framework for Recommendation

problem: Merging candidates from multiple interests maximizes relevance but often yields monotonous recommendations; systems need a controllable trade-off between accuracy and diversity in the final top-N.
method: An aggregation module optimizes a value function Q(u,S) that combines item relevance with a lambda-weighted pairwise diversity term (e.g., category dissimilarity), solved approximately with a greedy algorithm. The controllable factor lambda tunes accuracy vs. diversity, enabling personalized, balanced recommendations from the K·N retrieved items.
paper_title: Controllable Multi-Interest Framework for Recommendation

problem: Selecting EVOI-optimal comparison queries for Bayesian preference elicitation is computationally intractable for large item spaces because it requires enumerating items/slates and repeated deep retrieval.
method: Formulate EVOI/PEU as a differentiable computation over relaxed (continuous) query/recommendation slates and sampled utility particles, enabling gradient-based optimization (Monte Carlo approximation) in standard ML frameworks. After optimization, project hypothetical slate vectors to feasible items via a single deep-retrieval (with uniqueness), with variants (ContFree/ContReg/ContDeepRetr/ContAlter) and regularizers to keep solutions near the item manifold—scaling to million-item catalogs with state-of-the-art performance.
paper_title: Gradient-based Optimization for Bayesian Preference Elicitation

problem: Full-item comparisons are often cognitively heavy; finding EVOI-optimal partial (attribute-level) queries is a hard combinatorial search.
method: Extend the continuous EVOI framework to partial comparison queries under ceteris paribus semantics: optimize relaxed partial-item vectors in [0,1]^d with an L1 regularizer to induce sparse attribute sets and discretize to the top-p attributes; compute exact EVOI via deep retrieval during optimization (ContPartial), yielding informative partial queries that rival exhaustive/greedy baselines.
paper_title: Gradient-based Optimization for Bayesian Preference Elicitation

problem: It is unclear why contrastive learning improves graph-based recommenders and whether graph structure perturbation (node/edge/random-walk dropout) is actually necessary; representation collapse and popularity bias persist.
method: Conduct ablations on SGL with and without graph augmentations, visualize embedding distributions (t-SNE+KDE), and quantify uniformity; derive that optimizing InfoNCE minimizes inter-node cosine similarity, yielding more uniform representations on the hypersphere that implicitly mitigate popularity bias. Findings show the CL loss—rather than graph augmentations—is the main driver of gains.
paper_title: Are Graph Augmentations Necessary? Simple Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Dropout-based graph augmentations are inefficient (CPU-bound adjacency reconstruction) and fragile (dropping critical edges distorts structure), and offer no smooth control over representation uniformity.
method: Propose SimGCL: a graph-augmentation-free contrastive method that creates views by adding small, directed random uniform noises to node embeddings at each LightGCN layer. Noises have fixed L2 norm ε and share the sign of the base embedding (same hyperoctant) to retain semantics while injecting variance. Jointly optimize BPR + InfoNCE; ε provides a smooth knob to regulate uniformity. This yields higher accuracy, faster convergence, and lower training cost than graph-augmentation baselines.
paper_title: Are Graph Augmentations Necessary? Simple Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Learning task-specific policies from logged contextual bandit data across many related tasks is sample-inefficient when each task is optimized independently, and off-policy optimization must reason about counterfactual rewards safely.
method: HierOPO: a hierarchical off-policy optimization algorithm that builds pessimistic lower confidence bounds by factoring uncertainty in a two-level hierarchical Bayesian model—separating hyper-parameter uncertainty from conditional task-parameter uncertainty—and selects actions that maximize these LCBs per task.
paper_title: Multi-Task Off-Policy Learning from Bandit Feedback

problem: General hierarchical off-policy learning requires integrating over task and hyper-parameters, which is typically intractable for efficient deployment.
method: Instantiate the hierarchy with a linear-Gaussian model to obtain closed-form posteriors for task and hyper-parameters and closed-form LCBs based on per-task sufficient statistics (Gs, Bs) and the hyper-posterior (μ̄, Σ̄), yielding an exact and efficient implementation of HierOPO.
paper_title: Multi-Task Off-Policy Learning from Bandit Feedback

problem: There is a lack of theoretical guarantees that quantify the benefit of leveraging hierarchies in off-policy multi-task bandits.
method: Derive Bayesian per-task suboptimality bounds for HierOPO that decompose into a task term and a hyper-parameter term, proving improvement over non-hierarchical (flat) off-policy methods and convergence toward an oracle as the number of tasks grows.
paper_title: Multi-Task Off-Policy Learning from Bandit Feedback

problem: Extract-then-summarize QFMS systems either use binary relevance labels or ROUGE regression, which (a) ignores relative ordering among utterances and (b) is computationally prohibitive for long meetings.
method: A Ranker-Generator with two-stage learning-to-rank: (1) sample-wise pairwise ranking via a cross-encoder to prune unlikely utterances efficiently, and (2) global listwise re-ranking over top candidates to obtain a high-quality global order; the top-K utterances are then fed to a BART generator.
paper_title: Learning to Rank Utterances for Query-Focused Meeting Summarization

problem: Summarizers depend on the top-K inputs, but standard listwise objectives (e.g., ListNet top-1) do not directly optimize the quality of the top-K ordering.
method: A novel listwise loss that optimizes top-k permutation probability (generalizing ListNet beyond top-1) using KL divergence between predicted and gold ROUGE-based distributions, yielding more precise top-K selection and improved downstream QFMS quality with fewer parameters.
paper_title: Learning to Rank Utterances for Query-Focused Meeting Summarization

problem: IVF-style clustering for dense retrieval is lossy at low probe counts, missing relevant documents; probing more clusters sharply increases latency. Score-level hybrid systems (dense+sparse) require separate indexes and still suffer candidate-miss issues.
method: Hybrid Inverted Index (HI2) that indexes each document in both one semantic embedding cluster and a small set of salient-term inverted lists. At query time, dispatch to top-Kc clusters and top-Kt2 terms, merge candidates, and score with codecs (e.g., PQ/OPQ). This index-level fusion of lexical and semantic signals improves candidate coverage and achieves near-lossless recall at low latency.
paper_title: Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval

problem: Constructing compact inverted lists that reliably include relevant documents while keeping query-time overhead low requires accurate cluster/term selection and training aligned with retrieval quality.
method: Two selectors: (1) Cluster selector scores clusters via inner product with query/document embeddings. (2) Term selector scores document terms via BM25 (unsupervised) or a BERT+MLP scorer (supervised). Queries use lightweight dispatch (all terms for short queries; top terms by precomputed average score for long ones). A joint knowledge distillation objective trains neural cluster embeddings and the BERT term scorer by aligning selector-induced relevance distributions to a teacher’s scores (KL loss) with a commitment loss for cluster assignments, yielding substantial recall gains.
paper_title: Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval

problem: LLMs often underperform on personalized recommendation tasks because they do not explicitly infer user preferences from heterogeneous historical signals.
method: Zero-shot chain-of-thought prompting that first elicits natural-language reasoning over rich user histories (reviews + ratings + item metadata) before predicting a rating; ablations show reviews are critical and CoT substantially improves rating prediction over direct outputs.
paper_title: Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems

problem: ANN acceleration must deliver high effectiveness with low latency and modest memory across codecs, configurations, and embedding models without expensive query-time computation.
method: Efficiency-oriented design: compact per-doc term indexing (small KT1) and small Kc; query-side term dispatch avoids running BERT online by using cached average term scores; merge-and-rerank with PQ/OPQ; optional pruning of oversized term lists to bound posting lengths. These choices reduce evaluated candidates and achieve lower latency than VQ baselines and near-HNSW speed with smaller index size, while remaining robust across RetroMAE/AR2 and various index settings.
paper_title: Hybrid Inverted Index Is a Robust Accelerator for Dense Retrieval

problem: Smaller LMs lack strong reasoning ability for RecSys and zero-shot CoT is costly; how to transfer reasoning to efficient models.
method: Distill reasoning by sampling multiple diverse reasoning–rating pairs from a larger LM (via temperature), optionally filtering by agreement with ground truth (5-class/binary/1-off), and fine-tune smaller Flan-T5 models to produce reasoning then rating, yielding consistent performance gains and domain-dependent benefits from filtering.
paper_title: Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems

problem: Reasoning quality in RecSys is subjective and lacks gold references or scalable human evaluation.
method: Rec-SAVER: automatically generate post hoc explanations conditioned on ground-truth ratings, apply LLM self-verification to retain only explanations that support correct re-prediction after leakage scrubbing, and use these verified references to score model reasons with NLG metrics (BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR/BERTScore), showing strong alignment with human judgments on coherence and faithfulness.
paper_title: Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems

problem: Deterministic user/item embeddings and fixed Euclidean distances ignore uncertainty and can miss fine-grained preferences while violating triangle inequality.
method: Represent each user and item as a Gaussian (learnable mean and diagonal covariance) and measure preference with closed-form 2-Wasserstein distance between Gaussians, capturing both mean and uncertainty while preserving metric properties.
paper_title: Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation

problem: A fixed hinge-loss margin across training triplets is suboptimal and naive margin learning collapses margins toward zero.
method: Learn triplet-specific adaptive margins via a neural margin generator f(i,j,k;Φ) trained with bilevel optimization: inner step updates model parameters using adaptive-margin loss; outer step updates Φ using a fixed-margin loss through a one-step proxy gradient. Inputs s_ijk encode fine-grained element-wise relations; softplus enforces positive margins; reparameterization trick enables backprop through stochastic embeddings.
paper_title: Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation

problem: Distance-based recommenders often ignore explicit user-user and item-item similarities, leading to incoherent neighborhoods.
method: Augment the objective with two additional adaptive-margin ranking losses over similar user pairs and similar item pairs (neighbors mined via cosine similarity from the interaction matrix), explicitly pulling similar users/items closer in the latent metric space and training jointly with the user–item loss.
paper_title: Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation

problem: Federated rating prediction on resource-constrained devices either requires large shared models with on-device fine-tuning or loses collaborative filtering benefits under privacy constraints.
method: MetaMF: a server-side meta network that uses a collaborative memory (shared basis memory; user indicator → user embedding → collaborative vector) and a meta recommender to generate per-user private item embeddings and a compact MLP rating-prediction model. The small personalized model runs on-device; devices send gradients to update the server-side meta network, enabling CF without sharing raw data and keeping the on-device footprint small.
paper_title: Meta Matrix Factorization for Federated Rating Predictions

problem: Generating user-specific high-dimensional embeddings for a large item catalog is prohibitively expensive in parameters and computation.
method: Rise-dimensional generation (RG): the meta network first generates a low-dimensional item embedding matrix and a user-specific up-projection (rise-dimensional) matrix, then multiplies them to form high-dimensional item embeddings, reducing parameters from O(d·n) to O(s·n + d·s) with s << d, enabling scalable per-user embeddings.
paper_title: Meta Matrix Factorization for Federated Rating Predictions

problem: Are pure modality-based recommenders competitive with traditional ID-based models for regular and warm-item recommendation (not just cold-start)?
method: Conduct a fair, controlled comparison by replacing itemID embeddings with state-of-the-art text/vision encoders (BERT/RoBERTa, ResNet/Swin) under identical backbones (two-tower DSSM and sequential SASRec), losses, and samplers; train end-to-end including the modality encoder. Findings: with SASRec (seq2seq) MoRec matches/surpasses IDRec even on warm items, while with DSSM MoRec lags—showing the necessity of a powerful sequential backbone for MoRec.
paper_title: Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited

problem: Can recommender systems inherit performance gains from advances in NLP/CV foundation models (model size, architecture strength, pretraining)?
method: Systematic scaling and ablation: vary encoder size (e.g., ResNet18/34/50; Swin-T/B; BERT tiny/small/base), architecture strength (RoBERTa > BERT > OPT; Swin > ResNet; vs TextCNN/GloVe), and training regime (pretrained vs from-scratch). Results show generally higher recommendation accuracy with larger/stronger/pretrained encoders—especially for vision—demonstrating positive transfer to MoRec.
paper_title: Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited

problem: Are modality representations universal enough to be used as frozen features in industrial two-stage RS pipelines, or is end-to-end tuning necessary?
method: Compare two-stage (frozen modality features with/without deep MLP adapters) vs end-to-end joint fine-tuning within the recommender across text and image datasets. E2E markedly outperforms TS (TS often 25–60% of E2E for images), despite 100x higher training cost and risks like training collapse without careful LR scheduling; naive fusion of ID and modality does not help. Conclusion: modality features are not universal for RS; E2E adaptation is crucial.
paper_title: Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited

problem: CTR models often ignore explicit modeling of cross dense (numerical) features, instead using them only linearly, bucketizing (losing information), or naively concatenating with sparse embeddings.
method: Introduce a dense-feature cross layer that recursively generates high-order (up to l+1) cross terms and can approximate multivariate polynomials, enabling explicit, interpretable, and efficient high-order interactions for numerical fields.
paper_title: XCrossNet: Feature Structure-Oriented Learning for Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: Capturing pairwise interactions among many sparse categorical fields is either implicit (hard to explain) or parameter-heavy when enumerated explicitly.
method: Use an embedding + product layer that constructs a T-dimensional vector of order-2 cross features via weighted sums of all pairwise inner products, with low-rank factorization of weights (W_ij = Θ_i·Θ_j), plus order-1 weighted sums; this yields explicit and parameter-efficient sparse interactions.
paper_title: XCrossNet: Feature Structure-Oriented Learning for Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: Dense and sparse interactions are not jointly modeled and suffer from feature dimensionality imbalance, weakening CTR prediction.
method: Concatenate dense-cross and sparse-cross features and apply an additional cross layer to explicitly model inter-structure interactions, followed by an MLP for non-linear feature selection; adjust dense cross-layer depth to balance dimensions, improving accuracy and efficiency.
paper_title: XCrossNet: Feature Structure-Oriented Learning for Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: Single user embeddings fail to capture users’ multiple, evolving interests, and fixing the number of embeddings per user restricts expressiveness.
method: Cluster each user’s last-90-day action pins in the fixed PinSage embedding space using Ward hierarchical agglomerative clustering (auto-selects number of clusters) and represent each cluster by a medoid pin (not a centroid) to avoid topic drift/outliers, yielding multiple robust, interpretable user-interest embeddings.
paper_title: PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest

problem: At inference time, selecting which interests to use must adapt to recency and budget constraints without expensive full re-computation.
method: Compute a time-decay importance score per cluster and sample a small set (e.g., 3) of medoids for retrieval; maintain them with a two-pronged serving pipeline—daily batch inference over long history plus lightweight online updates from most recent actions—to balance long-term and short-term interests.
paper_title: PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest

problem: Billion-scale candidate retrieval for multiple user embeddings must meet strict latency and cost targets.
method: Deploy an ANN retrieval stack using HNSW indexing, refine the index by filtering near-duplicates/low-quality pins, and cache medoid IDs to reuse popular queries; together these optimizations cut serving cost (e.g., HNSW vs LSH, index refinement, medoid caching) while preserving high recall/relevance, validated by large-scale offline metrics and online A/B gains.
paper_title: PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest

problem: Masked language models like BERT are inefficient for cloze pre-training (score only masked tokens, pretrain/fine-tune mismatch) and cannot provide fast per-token likelihoods for all positions.
method: Introduce Electric, an energy-based cloze model that assigns a scalar energy to each input token in its full, unmasked context; define unnormalized conditionals and train with conditional noise-contrastive estimation using a small two-tower bidirectional noise model, yielding self-normalized scores and clarifying ELECTRA as a negative-sampling variant of this EBM formulation.
paper_title: Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models

problem: Standard NCE training for EBMs is computationally expensive because it requires separate forward passes per negative sample.
method: Propose an efficient NCE approximation that replaces k random positions with noise tokens simultaneously and computes the loss for all positions in a single transformer pass, leveraging a small k and a strong noise model to keep the approximation accurate.
paper_title: Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models

problem: Re-ranking outputs of ASR/NMT requires effective pseudo-log-likelihoods, but masked LMs are slow (n passes) and unidirectional LMs underperform.
method: Use Electric’s per-token energies to compute bidirectional pseudo-log-likelihoods for entire sequences in one pass, enabling fast n-best rescoring that outperforms GPT-2 and approaches or matches masked LM quality at a fraction of the runtime.
paper_title: Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models

problem: Generating visually guided binaural audio typically requires scarce, hard-to-record visual–stereo (binaural) pairs, leading to overfitting and poor generalization.
method: PseudoBinaural: create pseudo visual–stereo training data from easily available mono videos by (i) Mono-Binaural-Mapping that converts a mono source at any direction to binaural using spherical harmonic (0th/1st-order) decomposition to ambisonics and HRIR-based rendering with a virtual speaker array for reverberation; and (ii) Visual-Coordinate-Mapping that links pixel locations to spherical angles, enabling manual placement of visual source patches and linear mixing of multiple sources to form realistic pseudo pairs for training a mono-to-binaural network.
paper_title: Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios

problem: Learning correct source–location associations from pseudo pairs is difficult, especially with multiple concurrent sources.
method: Joint auxiliary audio-visual source separation during training: adopt Mix-and-Separate with APNet branches to predict individual STFTs of two sources while placing their visual patches at distinct image edges, improving the network’s ability to disentangle sources and learn robust audio–vision spatial correspondences for binaural generation.
paper_title: Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios

problem: Supervised binaural generation with limited real binaural data underperforms and lacks robustness across domains.
method: Use pseudo visual–stereo pairs as data augmentation alongside real binaural recordings during supervised training, which yields consistent improvements over Mono2Binaural/Sep-Stereo and better cross-dataset generalization.
